theses.title: NVDA demand durability
theses.statement: Datacenter GPU demand continues to outrun NVIDIA's ability to serve it.
theses.mechanism: NVIDIA's backlog and purchase commitments have grown faster than its shipment volumes, which is the signature of demand pulling ahead of supply rather than supply chasing weak demand.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: Backlog/RPO growth falls below shipment growth for 2 consecutive quarters.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex guidance and any broad AI-infrastructure spending slowdown that would let shipments catch up to backlog.

theses.title: Supply constraint binding
theses.statement: Advanced packaging + HBM — not wafers or demand — cap realizable merchant-GPU revenue.
theses.mechanism: CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity and HBM memory supply, not wafer starts or end demand, set the ceiling on how many finished GPUs can actually ship in a given quarter.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS/HBM lead times normalize to historical norms while demand indicators stay positive.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to announced advanced-packaging and HBM capacity additions from the packaging and memory vendors, which would loosen the binding constraint independent of demand.

theses.title: AMD credible second source
theses.statement: AMD converts its Instinct roadmap into material datacenter share.
theses.mechanism: AMD's Instinct MI-series roadmap and its hyperscaler design-win cadence determine whether it becomes a credible, at-scale second source in datacenter accelerators rather than a niche alternative.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters without a named hyperscaler MI-series deployment at scale.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to named hyperscaler deployment announcements and AMD's execution against its MI-series shipment cadence.

theses.title: Custom ASIC substitution
theses.statement: Hyperscaler custom silicon meaningfully displaces merchant-GPU demand growth.
theses.mechanism: Hyperscalers' in-house training and inference accelerator programs compete directly with merchant-GPU purchases for the same capex dollars, so their scale-up can substitute for merchant-GPU demand growth.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler capex mix shifts back toward merchant GPUs, or a named ASIC program is cancelled/delayed a generation.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to hyperscaler capex-mix disclosures and the shipment/deployment cadence of named custom-silicon programs.

theses.title: Pricing power persistence
theses.statement: GPU rental/spot price levels hold up despite added supply.
theses.mechanism: Sustained demand for GPU compute keeps rental and spot prices firm even as new capacity comes online, which indicates the market is not yet oversupplied.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: The D6 price track shows ≥15% decline across ≥2 providers within a quarter.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to the D6 rental price track across providers and to the pace at which new GPU capacity enters the rental market.

theses.title: Export control exposure
theses.statement: Export-control policy materially constrains addressable merchant-GPU demand.
theses.mechanism: Export-control restrictions on advanced GPUs limit the addressable market in China and other restricted destinations, redirecting or destroying demand that would otherwise be served.
theses.falsifiableTrigger: A full licensing regime rollback, or two quarters with no new policy action and no vendor-reported impact.
theses.sensitivity: Sensitive to new export-control rulemaking, licensing decisions, and vendor-reported revenue-impact disclosures tied to restricted destinations.
